summary: Set 023 Session 2: Cross-provider design-alignment audit on the Set 022 migration approach and the Set 023 fix designs (writer union from Session 1, reader fix planned for the originally-numbered Session 3). Routed a structured five-question prompt to GPT 5.4 ($0.10) and Gemini Pro ($0.01). Both providers concurred on the writer + reader design correctness, with non-blocking refinements: observability warn when array overrides missing ledger closeout, sharpened 'authoritative' doc language (whether-closed vs when-closed), extra reader fixtures (non-array completedSessions, stray out-of-range entry, non-final-session disagreement), and a follow-on ai_router 0.2.5 plan to clamp the writer union to [1..totalSessions]. Both providers independently raised the same third sharp edge on question (d): other progress-readers in the pipeline may still consult the events ledger without considering completedSessions[] (Gemini severity: critical; GPT severity: major). The strict spec gate ('Critical that invalidates the writer fix') did not trip — the writer fix is correct in isolation — but the operator was flagged for a scope decision and chose option (B): pause the planned reader fix and expand Set 023's scope to include a system-wide audit session. Spec restructured: totalSessions 3->4; new Session 3 'System-wide audit of events-ledger consumers' inserted; original reader fix renumbered to Session 4. Cross-provider verification of the audit-summary via gpt-5-4-mini ($0.025) found 1 Major: an internally contradictory sentence in the (b) spec-refinement (the summary claimed '[1,2,99].includes(4) is true' which is false). Fixed inline; no round-2 routed since the defect is a textual self-contradiction. Session 2 routed spend: $0.138 (GPT 5.4 $0.101 + Gemini Pro $0.012 + gpt-5-4-mini verification $0.025).

reason.specifics: Session 3 is a codebase-wide audit of events-ledger consumers added to Set 023 at the operator's request after Session 2's design-alignment audit. The work is a mechanical grep sweep across Python (ai_router) + TypeScript (extension) plus a per-consumer classification — already-correct, correct-to-ignore, or sharp-edge. Same Claude that just authored the Session 2 audit and the renumbered spec has the freshest context on what counts as a sharp edge under the Set 022 invariant and what would be a false positive (debug/history tools whose job IS to read the ledger directly). Effort-low stands: classification is contextual but each call is local. If Session 3's audit finds sharp edges, Claude can implement the fixes in-line; if it finds none, the session ships a doc-only audit-findings.md. Either way, Session 4's reader fix can then proceed with the system-wide concern formally resolved on disk.
